description: A person or care team 'nephoru' when they undertake care intended to change a health condition or its course. Medicine and therapy can form part of treatment, as can practical support or a changed environment. The verb records the intention; benefit, cure, authority, and the person's consent remain separate questions.
articulatory_notes: Place the tongue tip against the backs of the upper teeth for voiced dental 'n', carry the voice through the nose, and move into true-mid front unrounded 'e'. Bring the lips close without involving the teeth for unvoiced bilabial 'ph', let air pass between them, and round into stressed true-mid back 'o'. Make a tap or short trill with the tongue tip and finish with high-back rounded 'u'.
sound_symbolism: The dental nasal and bilabial breath at the opening give 'nephoru' an audible kinship with 'nepha' (medicine). The resemblance suits their close relation while the longer contour leaves treatment broader than a medicinal preparation.
usage_notes: The person or condition can be the object, or the verb can stand intransitively when the course is already known. The event-noun rule supplies treatment. 'theama' (care) is broader practical attention, while 'thekamu' (intervene) includes diagnostic and other acts without a treatment aim.

pillars.secular-buddhist-philosophy: Treatment responds intentionally to suffering and conditions without promising that every difficulty can be removed.
pillars.peace-linguistics-practices: Treatment stays separate from consent and authority, so a proposed course remains available for evaluation or refusal.

semantic_domains.activity: care intended to alter a health condition or course
semantic_domains.community: a treatment relation among a person and care participants
semantic_domains.physical: an action directed towards bodily or health change
